_note: API-sourced snapshot (offline replay). Regenerated by scripts/seed_demo_repo.py.

prs.title: ops: add gateway-level rate limiting (Nginx limit_req)
prs.body:
## Summary

Adds `limit_req_zone` rules to the Nginx gateway and **removes** the emergency
`django-ratelimit` decorators from `/search` and `/products`. Rate limiting is now
enforced in exactly one place: the gateway.

Motivated by the duplicated-limits incident in issue #27 — the "why" (the scraping
post-mortem, the two-layers-double-counting failure, the edge-vs-app reasoning) lives
there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `infra/nginx/api.conf`: `limit_req_zone` on `$binary_remote_addr`; `/search` capped at
  30 req/s per IP (burst=10); `/api/` global at 100 req/s per IP (burst=50)
- `infra/nginx/errors.conf`: custom 429 JSON body with `Retry-After` header
- `docker-compose.yml`: dev gateway service (`nginx:alpine`) with config hot-reload
- `requirements.txt`: remove `django-ratelimit==4.1.0`
- `search/views.py`, `products/views.py`: **remove** the emergency `@ratelimit` decorators
  (the gateway owns this now — do not re-add them)
- `docs/runbooks/rate-limit-tuning.md`: adjusting limits without a code deploy

## Load test (locust, 500 virtual users, 10s ramp)

| Scenario | Without gateway limit | With gateway limit |
|----------|-----------------------|--------------------|
| Peak req/s reaching Django workers | 11,800 | 312 |
| Worker pool saturation | Yes (100%) | No (28%) |
| p99 latency for legit users | 8,100ms | 420ms |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-27-duplicated-ratelimit-incident.md
- component: api_gateway
prs.author: ravi-menon

prs.title: chore: migrate orders + payments to PostgreSQL
prs.body:
## Summary

Replaces the MongoDB collections (`orders_v1`, `payments_v1`) with PostgreSQL tables.
All financial and order-state data now lives in Postgres with full ACID semantics and
DB-level foreign-key constraints.

Motivated by the data-integrity incident in issue #58 — the "why" (the chaos-test
corruption numbers, the compliance requirement, the tradeoff) lives there, not in this diff.
Mechanically: a maintenance-window migration (export Mongo → transform → bulk-insert
Postgres), not a dual-write — dual-write doubles the failure surface and leaves two sources
of truth.

## Changes

- `orders/models.py`: Django ORM models for `Order`, `OrderLine`, `Refund`
- `payments/models.py`: `Payment`, `PaymentEvent` tables with FK constraints to `Order`
- `migrations/0031_orders_to_pg.py`: Alembic migration scripts
- `orders/repositories.py`, `payments/repositories.py`: replace PyMongo queries with SQLAlchemy
- `docker-compose.yml`: swap `mongo` service for `postgres:16`
- `scripts/migrate_mongo_to_pg.py`: one-time data migration (run once, delete after)
- `tests/`: updated fixtures; added transaction-rollback tests for payment failure paths

## Benchmark (staging, 500k order records)

| Metric | MongoDB | PostgreSQL |
|--------|---------|------------|
| Order + Payment write (2-table atomic) | ~3.1ms (app-level "transaction") | 2.4ms (real transaction) |
| Refund with FK integrity check | N/A (app-enforced) | 2.8ms (DB-enforced) |
| Corrupt write rate (chaos test) | 0.4% silent bad state | 0% (constraint rejected) |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-58-mongo-transaction-integrity.md
- component: orders_service, payments_service
prs.author: daniel-osei

prs.title: feat: async email dispatch via Redis/Celery queue
prs.body:
## Summary

Moves order-confirmation email off the checkout request path onto a Redis/Celery queue.
The checkout endpoint now returns 200 immediately after the order is written; a Celery
worker handles SMTP dispatch with exponential backoff (3 retries, max 60s delay).

Motivated by the incident in issue #91 — the "why" (latency math, conversion cost,
the tradeoff we accepted) lives there, not in this diff.

## Changes

- `email_service/tasks.py`: new Celery task `send_order_confirmation(order_id)`
- `checkout/views.py`: remove direct `send_email()` call; replace with `send_order_confirmation.delay(order.id)`
- `email_service/worker.py`: Celery app config with Redis broker
- `reconciliation/jobs.py`: daily job to resend missing confirmation emails (the safety net)
- `docker-compose.yml`: add `celery-worker` and `celery-beat` services

## Benchmarks

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| Checkout p95 latency | 1,340ms | 541ms |
| Email delivery rate | 99.1% (sync) | 99.7% (async + retry) |

## Cross-References
- incident: ISSUE-91-blackfriday-checkout-latency.md
- component: email_service
prs.author: priya-sharma

issues.title: Legitimate users throttled by two rate-limit layers double-counting
issues.body:
## Summary

A scraping burst (~12k req/s from 3 IPs against `/search`) saturated our workers for 22
minutes; p99 hit 8.1s and conversion dropped ~31% during the window. As an emergency patch
we added `@ratelimit` decorators to the search and products views. They stopped the bleeding
— but within a day they created a second, self-inflicted incident: as we started rolling out
edge limits at the gateway, **two enforcement layers were counting the same requests
independently**, and legitimate users started getting rejected at roughly half the intended
allowance, inconsistently and unpredictably.

## What we measured

| Signal | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Scraping burst that triggered the patch | ~12k req/s from 3 IPs |
| Worker saturation during the burst | 22 minutes, p99 8.1s |
| Conversion drop during the burst window | ~31% |
| Effective allowance once both layers were live | ~50% of the intended limit |
| User-visible symptom | inconsistent rejections; a request "allowed" at one layer, rejected at the other |
| Reproducibility of the rejections | low — depended on which layer counted first |

The two layers had no shared notion of a request's budget. The app decorators counted one
way; the early gateway rules counted another. A user well under the *intended* allowance
could be rejected because each layer had independently spent part of their budget. False
confidence everywhere: each layer "looked fine" in isolation, the combined behavior was wrong.

## Root cause

We had **two rate-limiting systems** enforcing overlapping rules on the same traffic. Beyond
the double-counting, the app-layer approach was structurally wasteful: by the time a request
reaches a view decorator it has already opened a socket, completed TLS, been parsed, and
spawned a worker — we burn ~40ms of expensive compute just to say "no." At 12k req/s of
abuse, "rejected in the application" still saturates the worker pool. The emergency
decorators were a bandage, never the architecture.

## The lesson / what we decided

There must be **exactly one** place that enforces a request budget, and it must be at the
edge — the gateway — where an abusive request is dropped in <1ms before it touches a worker,
a connection, or any application resource. Two overlapping enforcement points is how you get
inconsistent behavior and false confidence; consolidating to one is how you get a single,
predictable budget per client.

Operational bonus: with enforcement in gateway config, changing a limit is an edit-and-reload,
no code deploy, no test cycle, no PR — oncall can adjust at 3am without waking a dev.

**Adding a throttle decorator or framework throttle class to a public endpoint is banned.**
The emergency decorators must be removed the moment the gateway rules land — leaving them in
is precisely what recreated this duplicated-budget incident. If you are tempted to add one,
you are looking at the wrong layer: write a gateway rule instead.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-19-gateway-rate-limit.md
- assumption: two overlapping enforcement layers double-count one client's budget; holds whenever a second rate-limit layer is introduced alongside the gateway
- component: api_gateway
issues.author: ravi-menon

issues.title: Silent data corruption in orders/payments under network partition
issues.body:
## Summary

Our chaos-test sprint surfaced a class of failure we cannot tolerate for money data:
under simulated network partition, the MongoDB write path for `orders` and `payments`
left records in an inconsistent state — an order recorded with no matching payment row,
and refunds that applied partially — with **nothing in the system complaining.** No error,
no alert. Just quietly wrong financial state that would have required manual reconciliation
to even find.

## What we measured

We ran the v2 data-layer redesign through a fault-injection harness (partition the app
from the store mid-write, repeat across the order + payment two-write sequence):

| Signal | MongoDB write path |
|--------|--------------------|
| Writes ending in inconsistent state (chaos run) | 0.4% |
| Orphaned records (order with no payment, or vice versa) | present |
| Partially-applied refunds | present |
| Surfaced as an error to the application | No — silent |
| Recovery | manual reconciliation only |

0.4% sounds small until you multiply it by payment volume. Every one of those is a
customer-trust event and a compliance problem, not an eventual-consistency footnote you
can shrug off.

## Root cause

The store gave us no way to make the two writes (order, then payment) atomic, and enforced
no integrity between them. Multi-document atomicity would have required the 4.0+
multi-document transaction API — extra overhead and *still* no foreign-key integrity — or
an application-level saga, which is far more machinery than the alternative. The "flexible
schema" we liked early on turned out to mean "the store will happily persist malformed,
half-finished financial state and tell us nothing."

## The lesson / what we decided

For transactional money data — orders, payments, refunds — correctness is not negotiable
and must be enforced **by the store, not by hopeful application code.** We want the store
to *reject* a half-written transaction, to refuse an orphaned record, to make the two-write
sequence all-or-nothing. The schema being strict is the feature: it is a contract the data
layer enforces on our behalf.

A standards-based audit trail (WAL-based change-data-capture) is also a hard requirement
for the upcoming PCI-DSS audit in Q3; the auditors expect a conventional setup, not a
bespoke change-stream explanation.

Tradeoff we accepted: migration scripts on every model change (auto-generated, ~30s of
review) and harder horizontal write-scaling. At ~400 writes/minute peak with comfortable
headroom, that scaling ceiling is years away.

**This decision is specifically for transactional financial data.** Product catalog,
search, and analytics event stores can absolutely use a document store. But if anyone
proposes moving orders or payments back onto a schemaless document store, they have to
disprove the 0.4% chaos-test corruption number first.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-31-migrate-to-postgres.md
- assumption: a schemaless store silently persists half-finished money state; holds until store-enforced multi-entity atomicity + integrity exists
- component: orders_service, payments_service
issues.author: priya-sharma

issues.title: Black Friday peak-traffic latency regression on the purchase path
issues.body:
## Summary

During Black Friday peak-traffic, the storefront purchase path degraded badly. p95
on the buy flow climbed to 1,340ms and held there for most of the high-traffic
window. This was our single worst conversion event of the year, and it traced back to
work we were doing *inside* the buy flow that did not belong there.

## What we measured

Profiling the slow traffic showed that ~800ms of the 1,340ms p95 was a single blocking
call: the buy flow was waiting on a third-party mail provider API before it could reply
to the shopper. The shopper sat staring at a spinner while we talked to an external
provider that had nothing to do with completing the purchase.

The business cost was real, not theoretical:

| Signal | Value during the window |
|--------|-------------------------|
| p95 on the buy flow | 1,340ms |
| Portion attributable to the blocking provider call | ~800ms |
| Drop in purchase completions past the 1.2s mark (from last quarter's A/B) | ~6% |
| Estimated lost conversion revenue over the window | mid five figures |

The A/B data from the previous quarter had already told us that completions fall sharply
once the buy flow crosses ~1.2s. Black Friday simply pushed us far past that cliff at
the exact moment traffic — and revenue at stake — was highest.

## Root cause

The mail provider call lived on the critical path of the buy flow. Any slowness on the
provider's side, or any traffic spike that lengthened our own processing, was paid for
straight away in shopper-perceived latency and lost conversion. The provider's
responsiveness is outside our control, yet it was sitting on the most revenue-sensitive
flow we serve.

## The lesson / what we decided

The mail provider call must come off the critical path. The shopper should get an instant
reply the moment the purchase is durably recorded; everything the shopper does not need
to wait for (notifying them by mail) happens afterward, out of band, with retry and
observability so a transient provider failure never silently loses a notification.

Tradeoff we accepted, eyes open: we give up the same-transaction guarantee that the mail
went out at the instant of purchase. A daily reconciliation sweep resends anything missing
older than a few minutes. We judged that loss of an in-band guarantee a fair price for
removing ~800ms from the most conversion-sensitive flow we own — and for no longer
being hostage to a third party's reply time during our highest-revenue traffic peaks.

**Do not move this work back onto the buy flow's critical path without re-reading this
incident.** The latency math and the Black Friday conversion loss are the whole point of
keeping it off the critical path. If someone argues the provider is "fast enough now," that
is an assumption to measure and ratify, not assume — the provider's latency is not ours to
promise.

## Cross-References
- resolved_by: PR-42-implement-async-email.md
- assumption: provider-on-critical-path costs ~800ms; holds until provider call latency drops materially or leaves the revenue-sensitive flow
- component: email_service
issues.author: daniel-osei

incoming.title: Simplify checkout — send confirmation email synchronously
incoming.text:
# PR #57: Simplify checkout — send confirmation email synchronously

**Author:** @new-contributor
**Branch:** simplify-email → main

## Description

The Celery + Redis setup for sending confirmation emails feels like overkill and
adds moving parts to deploy. This PR simplifies it: the checkout endpoint now sends
the confirmation email directly via SendGrid SMTP, inline, before returning. One
fewer service to run, easier to reason about.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/checkout/views.py
+++ b/checkout/views.py
@@ def checkout(request):
         order = create_order(request.user, request.cart)
-        # hand off email to the background queue
-        send_order_confirmation_task.delay(order.id)
+        # send the confirmation email directly — simpler, no queue needed
+        send_email_smtp(order.user.email, render_confirmation(order))
         return Response({"order_id": order.id}, status=201)

--- a/checkout/tasks.py
+++ b/checkout/tasks.py
@@
-@celery_app.task(bind=True, max_retries=3)
-def send_order_confirmation_task(self, order_id):
-    ...
```

incoming.title: Add rate limiting to the search & products endpoints
incoming.text:
# PR #61: Add rate limiting to the search & products endpoints

**Author:** @new-contributor
**Branch:** add-search-throttle → main

## Description

The `/search` and `/products` endpoints occasionally get hammered by scrapers. This PR
adds `@ratelimit` decorators directly on the Django views so abusive clients get a 429.
It keeps the limiting logic right next to the business code, which is easy to read and
tweak per-view — no extra infrastructure to configure.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/search/views.py
+++ b/search/views.py
@@
+from django_ratelimit.decorators import ratelimit
+
+@ratelimit(key="ip", rate="30/s", block=True)
 def search(request):
     results = run_search(request.GET["q"])
     return Response(results)

--- a/products/views.py
+++ b/products/views.py
@@
+from django_ratelimit.decorators import ratelimit
+
+@ratelimit(key="ip", rate="60/s", block=True)
 def products(request):
     return Response(list_products(request.GET))

--- a/requirements.txt
+++ b/requirements.txt
@@
+django-ratelimit==4.1.0
```

incoming.title: Throttle the orders & checkout endpoints in the app
incoming.text:
# PR #63: Throttle the orders & checkout endpoints in the app

**Author:** @another-contributor
**Branch:** throttle-orders → main

## Description

Following the same idea as the search throttling, this PR protects the `/orders` and
`/checkout` endpoints by adding DRF throttle classes / `@ratelimit` decorators on the
views. Limiting in application code keeps the rules close to the handlers and avoids
touching the gateway config, which most of us don't have access to.

## Diff

```diff
--- a/orders/views.py
+++ b/orders/views.py
@@
+from django_ratelimit.decorators import ratelimit
+
+@ratelimit(key="user_or_ip", rate="20/s", block=True)
 def list_orders(request):
     return Response(get_orders(request.user))

--- a/checkout/views.py
+++ b/checkout/views.py
@@
+from rest_framework.throttling import ScopedRateThrottle
+
 class CheckoutView(APIView):
+    throttle_classes = [ScopedRateThrottle]
+    throttle_scope = "checkout"
     def post(self, request):
         return Response(create_order(request.user, request.cart), status=201)
```
